front.title: EMILIA Protocol: Human-Oversight Profile for Autonomous and High-Risk AI Systems

abstract.t: Governing instruments for high-risk and autonomous AI require a human to remain meaningfully in control of consequential actions, but none specifies an artifact that proves it, after the fact, to a third party, without trusting the operator. This document profiles the EMILIA Protocol (EP) authorization receipt () as that verifiable human-oversight evidence layer. It defines how to apply the receipt primitive at authorization boundaries for both human-in-the-loop and human-on-the-loop control, a small set of OPTIONAL authorization-context conventions, and the relying-party rules that make "a human was meaningfully in control" a verifiable artifact rather than an operator-owned log entry. It introduces no new cryptography. The profile is scoped to evidence, not cognition: it proves the fact, scope, currency, and authority of human authorization; it does not prove the human understood, or that the decision was wise or lawful. It is a necessary, not sufficient, condition, and says so throughout. Primary applicability is civilian high-risk AI; defense and lethal-autonomy regimes are noted as further, secondary applicabilities.

section.name: Introduction and Motivation
section.t: Every governing instrument for high-risk and autonomous AI requires a human to remain meaningfully in control, yet none specifies an artifact that proves it to a third party without trusting the system operator. The lead applicabilities for this profile are civilian:

ul.li: EU AI Act Article 14 requires high-risk AI to be effectively overseen by natural persons who can interpret output, decline to act on it, and intervene or halt. (The AI Act, Art. 2(3), excludes systems used exclusively for military, defense, or national-security purposes; treat Art. 14 as a civilian applicability.)
NIST AI Risk Management Framework calls for documented, auditable human oversight.

section.t: The same evidence gap recurs in further, secondary applicabilities -- DoD Directive 3000.09's "appropriate levels of human judgment over the use of force," and the UN CCW debate on meaningful human control of lethal autonomous systems -- which are noted here only to show the profile's reach, not as its primary framing. Across all of them the unsolved problem is the same: when an autonomous system acts, the record that a named human authorized that exact action -- at the right scope, currently, under the right authority -- is a log the operator controls and could forge, backfill, or rubber-stamp. EP closes that gap; this profile makes the mapping explicit.

section.name: Control Modes and the Authorization Boundary
section.t: This profile does NOT require per-machine-cycle human approval (incompatible with machine tempo). EP receipts are issued at authorization boundaries -- the discrete points where a human grants, scopes, or renews autonomous authority:

ul.li: in_the_loop -- a human authorizes each discrete consequential action before it executes (one receipt per action; the system fails closed without it).

on_the_loop -- a human authorizes a bounded engagement envelope (action class, target/effect set, geofence, time window) within which the system may act autonomously, retaining a revocation/halt authority. One receipt authorizes the envelope; autonomy operates only inside it and only while unrevoked and unexpired.

section.name: Authorization-Context Conventions
section.t: For the autonomy domain the receipt's authorization context MAY carry: control_mode (in_the_loop | on_the_loop); and an authorization_scope with effect_class, target_set, geofence, and window {not_before, not_after}. These are bounds the relying party enforces; they do not expand the core receipt's guarantees.

section.name: Relying-Party (Enforcement) Rules
section.t: A relying party (Policy Enforcement Point) MUST act only on a valid receipt and MUST fail closed otherwise. For on_the_loop, each individual action MUST be checked against the authorized envelope (effect class, target set, geofence, window) offline; an out-of-envelope, expired, or revoked action MUST be refused. Continuous evaluation and revocation apply (the halt authority). Higher assurance regimes (for example a two-person rule) SHOULD use EP-QUORUM (m-of-n distinct humans). Where an action executes, the enforcement point SHOULD emit a post-execution receipt bound to the authorization (draft-schrock-ep-enforcement-point).

section.name: What It Proves, and What It Does Not
section.t: The profile proves the fact, scope, currency, and authority of a named human's (or quorum's) authorization of an exact action or bounded envelope, offline-verifiable without trusting the operator. It does not prove that the human understood the action, was uncoerced, or that the action was lawful, wise, or proportional. Display-fidelity is a client-conformance concern; comprehension and lawfulness are out of band. Meaningful human control is necessary, not sufficient, and EP supplies the necessary, verifiable part.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Over-trust is the dominant risk: treating the existence of a receipt as proof an autonomous action was legitimate. A receipt proves authorization at a stated scope, currency, and authority -- nothing more. Coercion of an authorized human and authorization without comprehension are out of scope of the cryptographic guarantees; device-bound user verification and display-fidelity requirements raise but do not eliminate them. Per-component considerations are normative in the referenced documents.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document registers the control_mode values (in_the_loop, on_the_loop) in the EP profile registry. It has no other IANA actions.
